Indiana.ob: Zone-RPO spread under Curt Cignetti — 11 personnel, condensed splits, heavy play-action and glance RPOs off wide zone.
Indiana.ot: Methodical by design: elite efficiency (#4 success rate) with almost no vertical variance (#114 explosiveness). Best red-zone offense in the country (#1 points/opportunity). QB room resets after Fernando Mendoza's departure — the system, not the passer, is the constant.
Indiana.db: 4-2-5 under Bryant Haines — aggressive simulated pressures, run-through linebackers, press-quarters behind it.
Indiana.dt: #1 stuff rate and #2 havoc in FBS: they win at the line and on early downs. The tradeoff is boom-bust — #108 at preventing explosives. Teams that protect and take shots have a path; nobody grinds them down.
s: Best situational football in the sport — red zone and 3rd down on both sides
Front-seven havoc travels anywhere
Coaching edge: Cignetti's staff wins the margins
w: Explosiveness allergy on offense caps blowout upside vs elite defenses
New QB after Mendoza — efficiency machine needs a new trigger man
Secondary can be beaten over the top when pressure doesn't land

Ohio State.ob: Pro-spread under Ryan Day with new OC Arthur Smith (ex-Falcons HC/Steelers OC, hired after Brian Hartline left for the USF job) — expect more 12/13 personnel, play-action, and a real run-game commitment layered onto the WR pipeline.
Ohio State.ot: Julian Sayin returns behind Jeremiah Smith and Carnell Tate — the highest-ceiling pass game in the country, now with Smith's NFL wide-zone/keeper structure around it. The 2025 season ended in a Cotton Bowl CFP loss; the offense, not the defense, was the reason.
Ohio State.db: Multiple front under Matt Patricia — disguised coverage shells, NFL-style game-planning, elite pass defense metrics.
Ohio State.dt: Top-5 in EPA allowed and pass efficiency defense in 2025. They rally-tackle and squeeze explosives; pressure comes from scheme more than raw blitzing.
s: Sayin-to-Smith is the best QB-WR axis in the sport
Back-to-back elite defenses with NFL scheme diversity
Two former NFL head coaches now coordinate both sides
w: First-year college OC install — NFL-to-CFB transitions are not automatic
OL play, not skill talent, decides their CFP ceiling
Run-game identity was still optional against elite fronts in 2025

Notre Dame.ob: Duo/counter downhill run game under Mike Denbrock — 12/13 personnel mixed with CJ Carr's rhythm play-action.
Notre Dame.ot: Even post-Jeremiyah Love, the identity is RB-led: gap-scheme runs, heavy sets, then max-protect shots. Carr showed a live arm as a first-year starter; they'll trust him more in 2026.
Notre Dame.db: Tite-front quarters-match — sound, positionally disciplined, built to erase explosives first.
Notre Dame.dt: Bend-don't-break with elite tackling. They trade some havoc for structure; screens and QB run are the historical soft spots.
s: OL/RB physicality wins November football
Carr's year-two leap is the highest-probability QB improvement in the top 10
Freeman's roster has no bottom-quartile position group
w: Independent schedule means zero bad-week amnesty
WR separation vs elite man coverage is still a question
Pass rush relies on scheme more than a dominant edge

Oregon.ob: Will Stein's spread — wide zone base with layered RPOs, motion at the highest rate in the conference, tempo shifts as a weapon.
Oregon.ot: Dante Moore distributes to a rebuilt-but-elite skill room. They sequence runs into shot plays better than anyone in the Big Ten; 3rd-and-medium is where Stein's script lives.
Oregon.db: 4-2-5 under Tosh Lupoi — heavy front movement, deep DL rotation, edge speed over bulk.
Oregon.dt: Explosive-play prevention is the calling card; they'll concede completions underneath and close on the catch. Havoc comes in waves with the DL rotation.
s: Best roster construction in the Big Ten — portal and HS classes both top-5
Moore's second season in-system
Special teams and field position consistently elite
w: Have not won the biggest CFP-stage game yet under Lanning
Interior DL vs power run games (see: physical B1G losses)
WR room turnover puts pressure on year-one transfers

Miami.ob: Shannon Dawson's one-back spread — tempo, quick game to the boundary, vertical seams — behind Cristobal's mauling OL.
Miami.ot: The portal answer arrived: Darian Mensah from Duke — the ACC's leading passer (3,973 yds, 34/6 TD-INT) who won the league title against them. Pair that with a top-10 line-yards OL and this is the ACC's most complete offense on paper.
Miami.db: Corey Hetherman's attack front — high sim-pressure rate, aggressive run fits, man-heavy behind it.
Miami.dt: Big havoc jump in 2025; they trade structure for negative plays. Vertical shots off play-action are the counterpunch opponents live on.
s: Elite OL/DL two-deep — Cristobal recruits the trenches like nobody in the ACC
Mensah is the conference's best returning QB resume
2025 CFP run (Fiesta Bowl win over Ole Miss) broke the January seal
w: Aggression = explosives allowed when pressure whiffs
Mensah's first year in a new building — Duke's spacing isn't Dawson's tempo
Expectations now assume CFP; anything less reopens the Cristobal questions

Texas Tech.ob: Mack Leftwich's tempo spread — Air-Raid DNA with a real run game, top-25 explosiveness, fastest-scripting offense in the Big 12.
Texas Tech.ot: Behren Morton's exit hands the keys to a portal/blue-chip competition. They push tempo (73.9 plays/g) and hunt shots; success rate (#61) lags the highlight reel — drive-finishing came from defense-gifted fields.
Texas Tech.db: Shiel Wood's 3-3-5 — tite front, pressure from every second-level angle. #1 EPA defense, #1 success rate allowed, #1 havoc in 2025.
Texas Tech.dt: The best defense money could build: bought the entire DL two-deep from the portal and it worked. They win 1st down, then tee off. Question is repeatability after NFL departures (Bailey, Rodriguez).
s: Reigning #1 defense with the resources to re-stock
Tempo + explosiveness stresses any defense's conditioning
Administration fully committed to paying for it
w: New QB in a system that needs one
2025 was aided by a friendly schedule draw — margins shrink
Can the portal-built model survive year-over-year turnover?

Georgia.ob: Mike Bobo pro-style — 12 personnel base, condensed formations, play-action keepers for Gunner Stockton's legs.
Georgia.ot: More QB-run element with Stockton than the Bennett/Beck eras. Explosiveness has been the missing gear two straight years; they manufacture chunk plays with tight-end seams rather than WR speed.
Georgia.db: Smart/Schumann multiple 3-4 — pattern-match zone, elite vs the run, linebacker-driven.
Georgia.dt: Still top-shelf on early downs, but 2025 showed uncharacteristic explosive leaks in the secondary. The front seven reloads annually; the back end is the retooling project.
s: Line-of-scrimmage depth on both sides remains SEC-best
Smart's floor: double-digit wins in every full season
Stockton's mobility adds a red-zone dimension
w: WR separation and vertical speed — two years running
Secondary explosives allowed trending the wrong way
Offense can go conservative in exactly the games that decide seasons

Utah.ob: Jason Beck's QB-run spread built on Devon Dampier — zone-read, counter bash, designed keepers 12-15 times a game.
Utah.ot: Dampier returning makes them the Big 12's most defined identity: 61%+ run rate feel with RPO glance routes off it. Passing efficiency spikes when defenses over-commit to the box.
Utah.db: Morgan Scalley's 4-2-5 — structurally disciplined, safeties downhill, almost never misaligned.
Utah.dt: Classic Utah: top-20 success-rate defense, low havoc variance, tackle everything. They'll trade completions for time-of-possession and red-zone stops.
s: Clearest identity in the Big 12 — they know exactly who they are
Dampier is the conference's most valuable non-Manning QB
Whittingham-era culture: never out-toughed
w: Ceiling depends on Dampier staying healthy through QB-run volume
Pass game is complementary — playing from behind is a problem
Skill talent is functional, not scary

Ole Miss.ob: Pete Golding's first year as HC after Kiffin's LSU exit — but the spread-tempo offensive core persists, now built around a returning star.
Ole Miss.ot: Trinidad Chambliss is BACK for 2026 after winning his NCAA eligibility appeal — the reigning SEC Newcomer of the Year who quarterbacked a 13-win, first-ever CFP season (Sugar Bowl MVP in the win over Georgia; season ended in the Fiesta vs Miami). Ex-Auburn five-star Deuce Knight waits behind him.
Ole Miss.db: Golding's own 4-2-5 system stays — multiple fronts, sim pressures, transfer-heavy DL.
Ole Miss.dt: The identity Golding built as DC: disruptive interior line, aggressive early-down run fits, press man in must-pass. He kept the defensive spine through the transition.
s: Chambliss chose Oxford over following Kiffin — the rarest kind of continuity win
Program-record 13-win CFP season as the springboard
QB room is two-deep with high-end talent (Chambliss + Knight)
w: First-year head coach following the program's identity engine
Lost OC-level play-calling brains to Baton Rouge
The league now has a full offseason of Chambliss film

Alabama.ob: Ryan Grubb's dropback pass game — five-out spacing, chunk throws to the intermediate middle, RB screens as run-game extension.
Alabama.ot: Ty Simpson's draft departure hands it to Keelon Russell (five-star, year two). Pass-first identity is set; the run game (#102 rush EPA feel in stretches) and short-yardage remain the friction points.
Alabama.db: Kane Wommack's 4-2-5 'swarm' — light boxes, rally speed, disguised pressure.
Alabama.dt: Elite in coverage, occasionally soft vs downhill gap runs. Special teams (SP+ ST -1.30, #103) actively lost them field position all season — a fixable, glaring leak.
s: Russell is the most talented QB in the building since Bryce Young
WR room reloads with five-stars annually
Wommack's coverage disguise ranks with anyone's
w: Special teams was a bottom-35 unit — inexcusable at this level
Short-yardage/goal-line identity unresolved
First-year starter at QB in Tuscaloosa expectations

Texas.ob: Sarkisian pro-style — wide zone marriage with play-action dagger/flood, 12 personnel sprinkled, Arch Manning at the wheel.
Texas.ot: 2025 underdelivered on the preseason #1 hype: OL regression and drops, not Manning, were the story. Expect more designed Manning movement and a rebuilt interior line in 2026.
Texas.db: Pete Kwiatkowski's 4-2-5 — light-box quarters, elite eraser safeties, pressure with four.
Texas.dt: Back end is the program's best unit: top-10 pass efficiency defense three years running. Vulnerable stretch: physical duo/counter teams that stay on schedule.
s: Manning's arm talent + second full year as starter
PK's secondary erases one side of the field
Roster depth built for a 16-game season
w: OL interior was the 2025 disappointment — retooled, unproven
Run-game identity wobbles when zone gets stuffed early
Pressure to deliver now is at maximum

Texas A&M.ob: Collin Klein's spread — Marcel Reed's legs as a base constraint, quick RPO game, shot plays off sprint-out movement.
Texas A&M.ot: Reed returning after the 2025 CFP run makes this a top-10 offense on paper: designed QB run in the red zone, improving pocket patience. WR depth thinned by the draft.
Texas A&M.db: Mike Elko's multiple front — heavy nickel, twist-game interior pressure, coverage rotation late in the down.
Texas A&M.dt: Elko's fingerprints: top-25 success rate allowed, disciplined vs RPOs. Edge rush production must be re-sourced after draft losses.
s: Reed is the SEC's best returning dual-threat resume
Elko game-days: consistently steal one possession a game
Trench classes stacked three years deep
w: Receiver room needs a portal hit to stay explosive
Edge pressure regression risk
Program still hasn't stacked two elite years in a row

USC.ob: Lincoln Riley's Air Raid tree — spacing and leverage masterclass, best screen game in the country, tempo when wounded.
USC.ot: Riley's system produces top-10 pass efficiency regardless of the trigger man; 2026 QB competition (post-Maiava room) decides the ceiling. Run game is opportunistic draw/counter, not identity.
USC.db: D'Anton Lynn's 4-2-5 — simulated pressure, split-safety shells, major 2025 improvement in structure.
USC.dt: From liability to league-average-plus in one Lynn year: fewer busts, better tackling. Still lean up front against B1G power run games.
s: Riley's pass-game design remains a per-play cheat code
Lynn defense trending up with his own recruits
Elite specialists and hidden-yardage gains
w: Interior DL vs Michigan/Ohio State-style power remains the litmus test
QB transition year in a QB-dependent system
Cold-weather November road identity still unproven

Vanderbilt.ob: Tim Beck's option-flavored spread — but the Diego Pavia era is over, and the offense was built bespoke around his chaos.
Vanderbilt.ot: Two years of program-best offense came from QB-run keepers, fourth-down aggression, and Pavia's off-script genius. The 2026 offense must be re-founded: expect the same run-first, possession-length identity with a more conventional passer.
Vanderbilt.db: Clark Lea's 3-3-5 multiple — his own defensive rebuild caught up in 2025: opportunistic, turnover-hunting.
Vanderbilt.dt: They won 2025 games with third-down disguise and red-zone stinginess rather than raw talent. Regression risk is real as the league re-calibrates to them.
s: Lea has institutionalized an identity Vandy never had
Fourth-down/analytics edge is the sharpest in the SEC
Two straight bowl seasons of belief and retention
w: Replacing Pavia isn't a position change, it's an identity change
Talent gap vs the SEC's top half hasn't closed
2025 turnover luck won't repeat

Oklahoma.ob: Ben Arbuckle's Air-Raid-with-legs — John Mateer's dual-threat volume, empty formations with QB-draw constraint.
Oklahoma.ot: Mateer confirmed his return for a senior year ('I need to be better' — his words). 2025 was uneven: 62% completion, 14/11 TD-INT through a broken throwing hand he played through after 17 days. Healthy hand + year two in-system is the bull case; the designed-run gravity (8 rushing TDs) never left.
Oklahoma.db: Venables' 4-2-5 — cheat-the-count sim pressures, elite 2025 (top-5 EPA allowed), speed everywhere.
Oklahoma.dt: The best Venables defense yet: havoc without busts. Replacing interior anchors is the annual tax, but the scheme is now fully installed.
s: Mateer returning removes the biggest variable — chemistry with Arbuckle is year three
Venables defense hit elite in year four as promised
The Sooners finally look built for SEC line play
w: Mateer must cut the interceptions (11 in 2025) to justify the billing
WR explosiveness thin past the top two
OL depth one injury from trouble

Penn State.ob: Matt Campbell era begins (8-year deal from Iowa State, replacing the fired James Franklin) — expect his identity: multi-TE pro-style run game, play-action, culture-first program building.
Penn State.ot: Campbell turned Iowa State from 3-9 into a perennial bowl program with less talent than he inherits here. The roster skews power-spread with a top-15 OL two-deep and deep RB room; run-first ball control while the new staff installs, opening vs Marshall Sept 5.
Penn State.db: The defensive front is the program's export product — edge/DT depth stays elite through the transition. Note: 2025 DC Jim Knowles left for Tennessee, so the back end gets a new voice too.
Penn State.dt: 2025 was top-10 in success rate allowed before the season unraveled. Campbell's Iowa State defenses were famously structured (the 3-3-5 revolution started there) — expect scheme discipline over blitz volume.
s: Campbell is a proven program-builder with a top-10 roster for the first time
Trench talent on both sides survives regime change
Fan/NIL infrastructure remains top-10
w: QB position is a genuine unknown in year one
Coordinator turnover on both sides in one offseason
Campbell has never coached a season under this level of expectation

BYU.ob: Aaron Roderick's spread — Bear Bachmeier's true-freshman 2025 revelation, QB run married to deep play-action.
BYU.ot: Bachmeier returning for year two is the Big 12's quiet superpower: they punished single-high all season. Physical, patient, and better on 3rd-and-4-to-7 than anyone in the league.
BYU.db: Jay Hill's 3-3-5 — boundary pressure looks, disciplined eyes, turnover manufacturing.
BYU.dt: Top-20 havoc without gambling: Hill's blitzes hit home because the back end holds. Size vs SEC-style OLs is the only structural question.
s: Bachmeier is the best returning QB story in the Big 12
Hill's defense creates 2+ takeaways a game feel
Roster continuity in the portal era — nobody retains like BYU
w: Skill-position ceiling is modest by top-10 standards
Front sizes get tested by the league's biggest OLs
Thin margin for error with a top-heavy schedule

Iowa.ob: Tim Lester's outside zone — 12/21 personnel, the slowest pace in the sample (60.8 plays/g), play-action boots off it.
Iowa.ot: Gronowski's graduation reopens the eternal QB question. Elite line yards (#15) and points/opportunity (#20) say the infrastructure works; they shorten every game and bet on special teams.
Iowa.db: Phil Parker's quarters — two-high shells, drop-eight in must-pass, tackling machine.
Iowa.dt: #8 in explosiveness allowed: nothing over the top, ever. Low havoc by design; they'll concede 5-yard completions for sixty minutes and win the red zone.
s: Parker is the best pure DC in the sport, year in year out
Special teams wins them a game a season outright
Identity so stable it survives any roster
w: QB play caps the ceiling — again
Explosiveness (#132) means zero comeback capability
Modern tempo offenses stress their substitution patterns

Tennessee.ob: Heupel's Veer and Shoot — extreme splits, vertical stems, tempo that forbids substitution.
Tennessee.ot: Post-Aguilar QB room is open; the system's floor is high (spacing does the work) but its ceiling is QB-dependent. Run game is inside zone vs light boxes the splits create.
Tennessee.db: New DC Jim Knowles (via Penn State, and the 2024 natty-winning Ohio State defense before that) replaces Tim Banks, who left for Auburn's staff after the unit collapsed to #92 in scoring defense.
Tennessee.dt: Knowles brings his safety-driven 4-2-5 — late rotation, creative sim pressures, and a track record of immediate top-10 turnarounds. The scheme upgrade is the single biggest coordinator win of the SEC offseason.
s: Knowles is as good as defensive hires get
Scheme guarantees explosive plays regardless of personnel
OL continuity best of the Heupel era
w: QB competition with no proven answer
2025 defensive personnel didn't suddenly get faster — year-one Knowles fixes structure, not speed
Wide-split offense struggles vs elite two-high discipline

Michigan.ob: Chip Lindsey's evolving pro-spread around Bryce Underwood — year-two leap incoming for the #1 2025 recruit.
Michigan.ot: 2025 was training wheels: heavy gap run game, defined reads for Underwood. Watch the vertical volume spike in 2026 — the arm talent demands it.
Michigan.db: Wink Martindale's pressure-man — highest blitz-rate profile in the conference, corners on islands.
Michigan.dt: Boom-bust by design: elite havoc, occasional explosive concessions when the pressure loses. They erase bad QBs entirely.
s: Underwood's ceiling is the highest of any B1G QB
DL two-deep remains championship-grade
Program identity (line play + special teams) intact post-Harbaugh
w: WR separation remains the roster's thinnest asset
Martindale gambles get punished by elite processors
Underwood's year-two leap is projection, not yet fact

Washington.ob: Jedd Fisch pro-style spread — Demond Williams' dual-threat base, movement pockets, layered crossers.
Washington.ot: Williams quietly finished 2025 top-20 in EPA/play: the legs are real, and Fisch's route tree gives him easy first reads. OL rebuild is the throttle.
Washington.db: Multiple 4-2-5 — coverage-first, rally tackling, modest havoc.
Washington.dt: Competent, not scary: they limit explosives and force long drives, but generate little negative-play pressure without blitzing.
s: Williams is a rising top-10 QB nobody outside the West watches
Fisch's QB development track record
Skill room speed sneaky-elite
w: Trench depth (both lines) below B1G contender grade
Pass rush needs a portal answer
Travel-heavy B1G schedule again

Missouri.ob: Drinkwitz spread — Ahmad Hardy's downhill volume behind heavy pre-snap motion, Beau Pribula-era RPO game.
Missouri.ot: Hardy led the nation in rushing as a sophomore; everything constraints off him. Austin Simmons arrives from Ole Miss to settle the QB room — an SEC-experienced arm to keep defenses out of nine-man boxes.
Missouri.db: Corey Batoon's 4-2-5 — spot-drop discipline, interior push over edge speed.
Missouri.dt: Top-25 success rate allowed in 2025; the profile is stout-vs-run, adequate-vs-pass. They keep games in the 20s.
s: Hardy is the SEC's best returning RB — full stop
Drink's game-planning wins toss-up games
Home environment consistently banks an upset
w: QB clarity needed before the identity fully clicks
Edge rush is ordinary
Ceiling historically caps at 10 wins — breaking through needs variance

SMU.ob: Rhett Lashlee tempo-RPO spread — wide splits, snap-count warfare, RB screens as extended runs.
SMU.ot: Kevin Jennings' era likely closes; the system has produced a top-25 offense three straight years with three different QBs. Expect the same: pace, spacing, and YAC manufacturing.
SMU.db: 3-3-5 pressure-sim front — transfer-heavy back seven, gambling profile.
SMU.dt: Havoc-rich, bust-prone: they'll win you three games with takeaways and lose you one with explosives. ACC offenses solved stretches of 2025.
s: System offense with proven QB plug-and-play
Dallas NIL base keeps the roster stocked
Lashlee's in-game tempo manipulation is elite
w: Defensive floor is the concern in big games
Line play still a tier below ACC's best
Post-CFP expectations meet a harder schedule

Auburn.ob: Alex Golesh's tempo, TE-friendly vertical spread arrives from USF — OC Joel Gordon came with him (his play-caller all three Tampa years; #2 national total offense in 2025).
Auburn.ot: Golesh offenses live in 12-personnel spacing at Veer-and-Shoot pace — the exact inverse of the Freeze stagnation. Staff assembled in 17 days: Kodi Burns (WRs, Auburn alum), Larry Porter (RBs), Tim Banks (safeties, from Tennessee). QB room portal-rebuilt (Deuce Knight left for Ole Miss).
Auburn.db: Continuity where it counts: DJ Durkin RETAINED as DC — the fronts, sim pressures, and personnel carry over intact.
Auburn.dt: Keeping Durkin means the defense skips the reinstall tax entirely. DL talent accumulation is top-10 nationally; the 2025 defense was better than its record.
s: Golesh's offense fixes the exact thing that got Freeze fired
Durkin retention = one-sided transition, not a teardown
Jordan-Hare in year-one-energy mode is worth a home upset
w: Full identity reinstall on offense — year-one variance is huge
QB is a total unknown after the Knight exit
SEC schedule shows no mercy to transitions

South Florida.ob: Brian Hartline era — Ohio State's OC/WR-whisperer took the job in December 2025. Expect a pro-spread with elite receiver development replacing Golesh's tempo-vertical system.
South Florida.ot: Byrum Brown's graduation plus the system change makes this a double reset, but Hartline is a Tampa-area legend recruiting his own backyard. Final season at Raymond James before the on-campus stadium opens in 2027 — momentum is real even in transition.
South Florida.db: The 2025 defense was aggressive, multiple, and turnover-hungry — how much survives the staff turnover is the open question.
South Florida.dt: USF punched above its weight through scheme and culture; the architect left, but the administration's investment (and Hartline's recruiting pull) suggests a reload, not a collapse. Grade the two-deep in September, not July.
s: Hartline is arguably the best WR developer in football, college or pro
Tampa recruiting footprint is genuinely fertile — and now has a closer
Administration showed it will invest after the CFP near-miss
w: First-time head coach replacing a proven one, with a new QB
System change on offense means year-one friction
FPI's #25 is a lagging indicator — treat with maximum skepticism
